challenge_summary: The architecture is directionally appropriate for a low-cost demo, but it overstates readiness in four places: recoverability is still unresolved, required application telemetry is missing, GDPR erasure is not designed into the data model, and key security/governance assumptions are treated as settled before governance discovery.
compact_for_parent: Pass 1 (comprehensive) | HIGH | 1 must_fix, 4 should_fix | Key: Backup/RPO unresolved; App Insights omitted; GDPR erasure workflow missing

findings.title: Table Storage backup gap is not actually resolved
findings.description: The artifact says the Step 1 backup finding is addressed, but the chosen architecture still accepts no automated backup for Table Storage while earlier requirements still imply recoverability. LRS durability is not a backup strategy.
findings.recommendation: Either revise the demo recovery requirement explicitly, or make recoverability part of the baseline architecture with scheduled export/backup or a data store that supports restore.

findings.title: Required application monitoring was dropped from the architecture
findings.description: The requirements call for Application Insights, but the architecture only includes Log Analytics. That leaves a gap in application-level telemetry and troubleshooting depth.
findings.recommendation: Add Application Insights to the architecture and handoff, or formally relax the requirement and justify a Log Analytics-only posture.

findings.title: GDPR erasure is not mapped to the chosen data design
findings.description: The architecture stores orders in Table Storage but does not define how customer PII is deleted or anonymized while preserving any retained business records.
findings.recommendation: Define a storage pattern and lifecycle for erasure now: separate PII from order facts and specify delete-versus-anonymize behavior.

findings.title: Public-networking assumptions are treated as final before governance discovery
findings.description: Public ingress and no-private-endpoint choices are handed to implementation as settled decisions even though governance discovery has not yet verified they are policy-compliant.
findings.recommendation: Mark these choices as provisional and add a fallback path for private endpoints and stricter network controls if governance requires them.

findings.title: Privileged staff access is still an app-only control
findings.description: Staff access relies on application-level allowlisting rather than an explicit architecture control or distinct trust boundary for privileged operations.
findings.recommendation: Define a stronger staff access design now: separate admin surface, verified role claims, and documented enforcement points beyond email allowlisting.
